speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 5. March 27th, 1996. An interview with Dr. Michael Okunieff. Could you tell me what happened on September the 1st, 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: On September 1st, 1943 was started the final liquidation of Vlina ghetto. A contingent of Lithuanian, Estonian, and German came in-- in the ghetto, surrounded the ghetto. And they started to-- they demanded to send the people to-- to Estonia [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] under the direction of [? Neuberger, ?] what was the head of Gestapo for the eastern side. And they were going-- first of all, they knew that is a resistance. I was a [NON-ENGLISH] for. I went out from the house. At this time, I didn't go in the [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] this mean hiding. And I-- I went to across the street to [NON-ENGLISH] to take a look. And suddenly I see over there the Lithuanian, and Estonian, Ukrainian surrounding a building and taking out some of our fighters. This-- I belonged at that time so-called to FPO. In Yiddish we say, Fareynikte Partisan Organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: United-- United Partisan.

speaker: interviewee
text: In English, it's UPO-- United Partisan Organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the head of that-- the head was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Abba Kovner at that time. Wittenberg was killed. Glazman went to-- to the woods, because he was forced by-- by Gens. And Abba Kovner took over. I-- at that time, I went to the main-- to the main [NON-ENGLISH] place. On the [NON-ENGLISH] six. I told them about it. So they send me to the main-- to the main-- to the defense front line. But [NON-ENGLISH] 11. [NON-ENGLISH] 11 was-- I'm sorry. [NON-ENGLISH] 12. [NON-ENGLISH] 12 was on the corner main. And was leading-- you had to go in into the ghetto through [INAUDIBLE], and what was facing to that our-- our defence area. [INAUDIBLE] we're notice in here the German came closer to us. They was going back and forth. [GERMAN] with screams in here to go-- for the Jews to go out. And to just that they will be able to transfer them to Estonia. But they didn't go to us-- to our street. They knew that we are-- that we are over there armed. They had a good [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] information. Intelligence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Intelligence. So finally they moved out. And we saw that this is over. They came in with a strong contingent in the afternoon as they approached our-- our defense area. And they-- they probably about 12 or 14 of the German with Lithuanian, like in a [INAUDIBLE] in-- in a long line against the wall. And they started.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came in close to the other [NON-ENGLISH] that put in dynamite. So they tore up the building. And they let them out. [INAUDIBLE] the people are starting to run out from-- Some were killed, and some started to run out. But still away from our defense area. Finally, they came in closer. I was in our defence area. We [INAUDIBLE] onto the lower part of a [NON-ENGLISH] with-- with about 30 people. And I was on the second floor with maybe 15 or 18 people. And we-- we had [NON-ENGLISH]. But I mentioned before, he was the head of the group. What-- what's main objective was really to go to the woods. But instead of-- instead of it, he put the resistance. It took-- it took the-- he pulled out the [NON-ENGLISH] grenade. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: The pin.

speaker: interviewee
text: The pin. Threw it. And it didn't explode. He shoot It. He shoot. And it went, and didn't kill. Suddenly, we see a brush of fire. And I am in front of him. Suddenly, I see somebody is falling in front of me, and the-- and the whole ceiling is covered-- covered it's-- and it's from the ceiling to the paint is falling on your face in pieces. And here it went on my face. And I was covering with blood. And the thing is I looked in here. He's dead. I didn't even realize that he's dead when we went in there. In the meantime, I hear from down below, from [NON-ENGLISH] what was under Abba Kovner, Kinder! Let's run! We are surrounded. So she-- she ran away. I lost myself. I don't know why we're in here. So I went and hide myself. This was a infirmary-- [SHAKING HEAD] a school. They had the special tables for-- for the children. I hide myself under the table on the first floor. And before I notice, I see Germans. It's a Lithuanian came in, threw in some dynamite, and destroy the building.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the same building where Sheinbaum was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was destroyed the building. So it's the same Germans. Stop, cross, on-- on the roof. Behind me I could almost hear the-- the roof moving around with that. This will happen to any Jew that puts a resistance. [GERMAN] I shoot it in air with-- with his-- with gun. I don't know. Luckily, it didn't hit me. Anyways, it was already late. That he would-- they pulled out from-- from the ghetto. I went to the infirmary. They cleaned me up. And I was bleeding and covering when all this happened. And this-- this is the resistance. The other resistance-- as I said, it's enough. I cannot take it anymore. OK. So I decided in here, but I didn't have arms. So I had to get arms. I had 1,300-- 13,000 mark. And I found out that I can get some arms in some place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you have the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, that this, like, from-- from friends. 7,000 was not much. But for arms you could get anything in here. So I went out from the-- from the ghetto. And before you notice, I was brought-- that the [NON-ENGLISH], that a Pole follows me and says, from ghetto? Jew? Juden? And before you know this, he got me to count the money and brought me to Gestapo. And Gestapo in here, to make it short, is in here violent Gestapo. No Jew ever survived. They brought in guns and, in the dungeon Weiss brought him in. Shoot him. He comes out and says, [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Weiss was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Weiss was the head of Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you see guns brought in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not see guns brought in, because there were Polacks. We were in a different room. But when he came out, he took us out, and he was saying, told me-- told to his-- to his-- to his-- inferior--

speaker: interviewer
text: Subordinate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Subordinate. Now like a miracle, instead of killing us, to sending to Ponary, to send us back to the ghetto. Why? Because he wanted to calm down the ghetto. And now, a couple days later, they liquidate the ghetto. But we had already the feeling when they will in here, the night before. Luckily, the-- I and Kronick-- he's-- he lives in New-- in new York. We jump over the roof near-- near the church. We go to the second-- the second ghetto. We sleep over. They surround the ghetto for two days. And they take out till the last one. I wanted-- I took-- I was-- before I left, I asked my brother if he wants to go. And he just jumped through from the roof. He sprained his leg. He said, you go. I-- I will handle this. We can-- can do it. Anyway, he didn't make it. He went-- he went to [INAUDIBLE]. He went to Estonia. And over there he was killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm back. Now I don't have-- I'm alone in the world. Second ghetto, the ghetto is destroyed. What to do? How did you survive the-- the round-up in the second ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the second ghetto-- in second ghetto was empty. It was already destroyed. So the thing is, we slept overnight-- slept overnight-- for two nights. We don't move. On the third night, we didn't have food. So we-- I decided to go to [? Kailis. ?] I went into [? Kailis. ?] I thought, this is the only thing outside. You cannot survive. Under darkness of night-- in spite of the curfew, because they usually on curfew if they see somebody, they killed. We went to the [? Kailis ?] and said--

speaker: interviewer
text: And [? Kailis ?] was--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Kailis ?] was the-- the furs-- fur area. They were making furs for the army. Furs. The fur-- fur stuff. You know, the winter--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uniforms.

speaker: interviewee
text: --uniforms. So this was the area. But the Germans still kept it, because first of all, it was the winter, and they needed in here the special-- the warm uniforms. They put over there some, about 1,000 or whatever Jews. And they were making the uniforms, the warm uniforms for the army. So they took our some of the specialists in here to [? Kailis. ?] As I said [INAUDIBLE], and they survived. Two areas. There was [NON-ENGLISH] and [? Kailis. ?] It was still over there. In [NON-ENGLISH] 1,500 people. In here it was probably a thousand or 1,200. I don't remember how many This was from all that would be able to remain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the [NON-ENGLISH], what were they doing? What work?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [NON-ENGLISH] they were preparing the arms for the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Arms.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Now in here in [? Kailis. ?] But I knew already I heard that over there the resistance went through the-- through the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sewers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sewers. From and are organizing organize in [NON-ENGLISH]-- in-- in [? Kallis. ?] And from over there they are planning to go. I came in over there, and they told me, you cannot go, because you don't have the arms. You don't have arms.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from over there I went to my-- the old building. And I knew it that I talked to a doctor. The doctor is my cousin. And she was a big shot over there. She said, you know my-- my father. He had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a revolver in the place. And she told me where I'd go. And if maybe if you can find it, you can join the partisan. This was the original house that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But remained only some skeleton. Everything it was broken in there. Anyway, I went over there. And suddenly, I hear the dog. And in here a man comes in. I thought, this is it. It's going to end here. Finally, I found the same [NON-ENGLISH] which was before for years, but in our house. I said, yeah, I can give you if you will give me $15,000 I-- I found the--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the Pole. This was the Polish administrator.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish. Yeah. No, not the administrator.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a watchman. This was a watchman, administrator, and in the-- in the other. So the thing is, anyway I go into [NON-ENGLISH] again back to [? Kailis. ?] And finally I got from [NON-ENGLISH] fifteen-hundred-- $15,000 marks. I bring him in. He gives me the-- the revolver. I bring back-- back the revolver to-- to [? Kallis. ?] And take away from his revolver. He says, you cannot go. It's not your money. Anyway, the thing is I saw them in the middle of the night. [NON-ENGLISH] in the twilight. It was snowing. How the whole group goes out. I jumped through the-- through the barbed wire. Even with the [INAUDIBLE] I and Kronick, and we're going on the way. Anyway, I was following them for eight miles. Until we went in in here, and I said, [INAUDIBLE] you let me join it. And in here I'm with the partisan now. Now as soon as we're reaching the partisan, they don't really wanted to let us in. Over there in the partisan at that time was-- was a captain. What-- he was trying by-- from airplane or whatever in-- in 1942. And he organized the group. Asked me if I have an arm. And I did not have an arm. But I was with the group. Anyway, our group was close to 300 people. Some were armed. So we-- we decided even if he talks or not, we decided to make our own group. And the head of it was Abba Kovner. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name, [NON-ENGLISH]. Fight. Translation in-- in English, [NON-ENGLISH]. There was [NON-ENGLISH]. What is that, a [NON-ENGLISH]? It was the [NON-ENGLISH] to-- for victory. And it was [NON-ENGLISH], for to make-- to take courage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how many in each group?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about in ours was 100. And the rest of them was probably 75. And we start in here. The first-- the first encounter with what I have was-- is in here, is they told me to take the-- to take the-- the horses back. So it was [NON-ENGLISH], this means I got the food, and they brought in the-- brought in the-- the supplies. And we had to bring back-- back-- the-- the horses and the wagons back to the people what we took-- what we made the requisition. Second requisition. We didn't pay for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: From a peasant village.

speaker: interviewee
text: Peasant, yeah. I brought in. We come into the country-- on the beginning of the village and suddenly [CRASHING SOUNDS] from there-- from a bullet. I jumped off from there-- from the wagons, and we go back. And we start to run back on-- on foot. Anyway, next day the German came in. Goes-- go through our-- to our area. The captain made an ambush. He killed-- killed one German. And since that times, this was our area. And gradually it was starting. We have to develop-- what I heard yesterday-- we developed this psychology of war. We had-- we had psychology of respect for human dignity. The-- the Goyem have a respect for-- for power. And this is Nietzsche. Nietzsche in here. War is the ultimate achievement of improvement of human life. And so the thing is gradually you have the transformation. Next, a few days later, they send us to cut-- cut the electrical poles. And a few days later, finally on the six- six weeks, eight weeks you are already graduating to put some mines. And now we can tell a few stories in order to bring it in. [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is this is a hamlet or a village what was on the-- on our road. And a few days before, they killed five of our-- of our-- of our boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: People from the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No. They especially brought the German to put a-- to find out. You see, we had intelligence. And we found out what-- what is-- what's going on. So first of all, we-- first of all made safe for the Jews what are running away from the ghetto. Why? Because if somebody will expose them to German used to come in, or to burn his house, or even to kill him. So before you notice now the Jews could almost have a freedom over there to move.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second thing is in here. But now-- but now we have our-- we have already causalities. We decided already to-- to even up. And every single time we were going over there, they were shooting on us. Who was it? This was army [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, in between the Polacks, they had two. Armia [NON-ENGLISH] and Armia Krajowa;. Armia [NON-ENGLISH] is-- is the-- is the folks army.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Polish-- the Home. The Polish Home Army. The home [? party. ?] This one was with us. This one wasn't shooting with us. We were working with them. Armia Krajowa; is-- was under the direction of land army. This was under the influence of London. And they were rabid anti-Semite. Why? Well, first of all, they didn't want-- want us to burn their-- [NON-ENGLISH] from the Polish the nobility, their houses, because the German were over there. And we had to burn, it because--

speaker: interviewee
text: Second thing, their-- their final-- final-- [INAUDIBLE] final goal was to have a free Poland without the Russian. So the thing is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the--

speaker: interviewer
text: With the village then? With Niewieniance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Niewieniance. We come into the village. We surround the village. We had all our [INAUDIBLE]. You see, all [INAUDIBLE] we agreed that all fighters what's in here in our [INAUDIBLE] we had a hundred people probably. About 60 or 70. So probably about 70. They were armed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the thing is, we're surrounded in here. We-- every a hundred had about four people. We're going in every-- in every house. We have four people, surrounded. We go in. This was just in the morning. And-- and we just put up the fire. We're taking-- we're making a fire, we threw it. And we were burned, and we killed all of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in front of the-- of the village and I was the second [NON-ENGLISH]. This mean the second-- I had a machine gun. The machine gun. I was carrying [NON-ENGLISH]. And I had the-- I had the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The bullets. The bullets for the machine gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bullets, yeah. Grishka Gervich was the first. He was carrying the [NON-ENGLISH]. Now you had-- in addition I had a-- a revolver.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, the thing is we went over there, and we took, afterwards, we took as a-- as a prisoner a guy with a white horse. We thought maybe this is a [INAUDIBLE] from the-- from the village. We took him across-- across the little-- not a river. But how do we call this? A little stream.

speaker: interviewee
text: A stream. We made especially-- especially artificial bridge. What we went in here. But it was very narrow. We could barely go through. And we-- as soon as we passed by the creek, we could-- we took it easy. And we started to shoot just to show that we are through. But [? Grishka ?] by mistake picked up the-- the [INAUDIBLE]. And what happened in here, one of the bullet just-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Just right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he hit a friend, and he started to bleed. And he died. In the meantime, in this commotion-- in this commotion we have the guy, the chief of the village. What was the chief of the Army [NON-ENGLISH]. He was the head of it. He started to run away. And we started to shoot on him. And he got away-- got away. Now this is one. The second-- the second thing what I remember-- I give you all these Stories-- Is about a friend of mine, Yochanan--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it [? Boncka? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This is not [? Boncka. ?] Yochanan [CHECKING PAPERS] Maggid. He was a group commander. And in the later part of-- of the-- of '43. No. It was already '44, I believe. The food was hard to get. We cleaned up a little bit the villages near the front. The German burned them. And we had to make [NON-ENGLISH]-- this means to gather our food-- quite-- quite far away. We had to go miles and miles. And the thing is, and we used to send them. Our boys were sent out all over to-- to burn and to-- to destroy the trains. And from Moscow, we started to get arms from above they were throwing us. We started to be already respected. And not only this, they started to send us leaders. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, the top people from-- from Russia [INAUDIBLE], they didn't give us [? your ?] freedom. They told us to we cannot have a free time. And at that time-- one thing, I want to-- I want one of the stories to-- to describe to. And we went about-- about 6, or 5, 6 kilometer from Vlina. And in a village. And we made it [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were about ready to go back. And when we started to go back, suddenly from nowhere on the-- on the mountain we see a group of the Armia Krajova from the-- from the Polish. Maybe-- maybe we were about 18. They were about 35, 36. And they started to shoot. I was at that time the first [NON-ENGLISH]. I had the [NON-ENGLISH]. I had the machine gun. Before I notice, all of the group went to the woods. They went already with the horses, with all-- with all this stuff. And they were almost ready to go. I want to remain by myself. I did not see them how-- how they left it. And by remaining by myself, they-- they neared to a kill. The army [? crew. ?] And when we get-- [INAUDIBLE] a machine gun, it was really trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yohanan Maggid was the head under the cover of-- of a wagon. He came into me. He hit me over my back, and said, what are you doing? So I before I notice I said, the thing is-- Come! Come! I followed him. And this way he saved my life. And this is-- this is the way Maggid. I met him in Israel and I thank him for it.
